one_line_takeaway: The paper formally connects LLM 'sensitivity awareness' (adherence to RBAC-style access rules) to differential privacy and attribute inference via privacy games, and shows LoRA fine-tuning on ADI-benchmark annotations can boost 4-bit quantized Qwen3 models' sensitivity-awareness correctness by up to 21.7 percentage points, surpassing larger full-precision open- and closed-source models while largely preserving general capabilities.
key_finding: LoRA fine-tuning substantially improves SA: overall correctness rose from 0.5527 to 0.7698 for Qwen3-8B (+21.71 percentage points) and from 0.6030 to 0.7372 for Qwen3-14B, with the largest gains in the adversarial 'malicious' (Qwen3-8B: 0.1453→0.5669) and 'lying' (0.0093→0.3387) categories. The LoRA Qwen3-8B model (0.7698) outperforms all evaluated closed-source models (best: GPT-5 nano 0.7473) and all open-source full-precision baselines, and is described as on par with the closed-source Grok-2 reported in the original ADI paper. Notably the smaller 8B LoRA model outperforms the 14B LoRA model overall, suggesting smaller backbones may be more receptive to SA-specialization. Both LoRA models show a drop in the 'supervisor' scenario correctness relative to their base models (Qwen3-14B: 0.9880→0.8453; Qwen3-8B: 0.9240→0.8867), attributed to the supervisor scenario rewarding high recall (always-share) behavior that SA fine-tuning trades off against. On general-capability benchmarks, the Qwen3-8B LoRA model loses only marginal IFEval performance (instance-level 0.4173→0.4161; prompt-level unchanged at 0.2699), at worst 3.3 percentage points on GSM8K-Platinum (flexible extract 0.8933→0.8602), but a larger 9.3-point drop on BIG-Bench Hard exact match (0.7689→0.6756).
core_contribution: The paper (Fazlija, Olatunji, Kudenko, Sikdar; L3S Research Center / University of Luxembourg) makes two novel contributions on top of prior work (Fazlija et al. 2025, 'Access Denied Inc' / ADI, which introduced the concept of Sensitivity Awareness, SA, and its benchmark). First, it theoretically formalizes SA via a privacy game (Game 1) and proves a formal connection between SA and Differential Privacy (DP): Lemma 1 shows SA advantage is upper-bounded by Attribute Inference (AI) advantage (SA ⪯AI); Theorem 2 gives a general lower bound on achievable SA advantage from statistical correlation between non-sensitive context φ(z) and sensitive attribute π(z); Theorem 3 gives an (ε,δ)-DP-based upper bound on SA adversary advantage, Adv_SA(A) ≤ Adv_AI(A) ≤ (e^ε−1+2δ)/(e^ε+1). Second, the paper develops and empirically validates a LoRA-based supervised fine-tuning recipe to improve SA in 4-bit quantized, locally-deployable Qwen3 models (8B, 14B), using the 30,897 correct annotations from the original ADI dataset (75% chain-of-thought + 25% output-only) as SFT signal, LoRA rank 32 / scale 32 on attention+MLP projections via the unsloth package.
threat_model: Adversary A interacts with a corporate LLM-based data-retrieval/RAG system through role-based access (RBAC0: users, roles, permissions, sessions) and tries to extract a sensitive attribute π(z) about a target employee z that their role/permissions r*/p* do not authorize. The adversary observes only non-sensitive context φ(z) and queries a black-box oracle O(·) that retrieves documents via RAG, produces an unfiltered model answer, then applies a GuardRBAC0 filter before returning a response. Game 1 formalizes the adversary's win condition as correctly guessing π(z) after a coin-flip determines whether the sensitive value is redacted in the retrieval pool. This threat model is inference-time access-control leakage (insider/authorized-but-over-scoped querying and adversarial prompting, including 'malicious' and 'lying' request categories in the ADI benchmark), not training-data extraction or external jailbreaking of a public model.
limitations: General-capability trade-off evaluation is conducted only for the 8B model (the one 'substantially affected by fine-tuning'), not the 14B model, limiting generality of the utility-preservation claim. The theoretical DP bound (Theorem 3) is stated but the paper does not empirically instantiate or measure an actual (ε,δ) for the fine-tuned models -- it is a formal/analytical contribution, not a certified privacy guarantee applied to the trained systems. Comparison between the local 4-bit quantized Qwen3 models and other models evaluated via OpenRouter at full precision introduces a precision/deployment confound. The drop in 'supervisor' scenario correctness is acknowledged as a caveat/trade-off rather than resolved. The paper's own future-work section flags that more sophisticated fine-tuning strategies and unstructured-data SA scenarios remain unexplored, and that DP-based verification of SA is left to future research rather than demonstrated here.

datasets_used: Access Denied Inc (ADI) benchmark and its 30,897 correct SFT annotations (Fazlija et al., 2025)
Three newly generated ADI-pipeline mock corporate evaluation sets of 3,500 questions each (10,500 total), created to avoid data contamination
quantitative_results: Qwen3-8B base overall correctness 0.5527 -> Qwen3-8B LoRA 0.7698 (+21.71 pp)
Qwen3-14B base overall correctness 0.6030 -> Qwen3-14B LoRA 0.7372
Supervisor-category correctness drop after LoRA: Qwen3-14B 0.9880 -> 0.8453; Qwen3-8B 0.9240 -> 0.8867
GPT-5 nano overall correctness 0.7473 (best among closed-source comparators, still below Qwen3-8B LoRA 0.7698)
Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite 0.6330; Amazon Nova Lite-v1 0.6330; Llama 4 Scout (17B) 0.5819; Phi-4 (14B) 0.4931; Mistral Nemo (12B) 0.6025; Llama 3.1 (8B) 0.4326 overall correctness
Qwen3-8B base vs LoRA on BIG-Bench Hard exact match: 0.7689±0.0046 -> 0.6756±0.0049 (-9.3 pp)
baselines_compared: Qwen3-14B and Qwen3-8B 4-bit quantized base models (unsloth variants)
Grok-2, cited as the best-performing model from the original ADI study (Fazlija et al. 2025), used as an external reference point rather than re-evaluated in this paper
